questions.prompt: What does instance swap property do?

choices.text: Allows the instance user to swap a NESTED instance (e.g., the icon inside a button) from a curated list of allowed components, configured in the right panel without entering the component. Setup: select the nested instance → 'Apply property' → instance swap → name (Icon) → set 'Preferred values' to your icon library components. Instance users see a dropdown of only the curated icons — not every component in the file. Why curate: without preferred values, the swap picker shows every component in the file, often 100s, slow and confusing; with curation, designers see 20-30 relevant choices. This pattern is how design systems achieve flexibility — the Button component supports any icon from the Icon library without the designer needing to know Figma's component structure, just pick from the dropdown. Real-world: Cards with swappable avatars, Buttons with swappable icons, ListItems with swappable thumbnails. Combined with variant + text + boolean properties, instance swap completes the four-property toolkit for production-grade design systems.

questions.explanation: Swap nested instance from a curated list. Configure preferred values to scope the picker; right panel becomes a dropdown.

questions.prompt: Your Card component has 25 properties; the right panel is a wall of toggles. Designers complain it's overwhelming. What's the fix?

choices.text: Split the Card into smaller components. 25 properties means the Card is doing too much — likely it bundles avatar, action area, tag system, expansion behavior, and density into one mega-component. Split into focused components: CardBase (just title + body + variant for layout), CardWithAvatar (extends with avatar slot), CardWithActions (extends with action buttons), CardExpandable (extends with expand state). Each has 5-8 properties — manageable. Designers compose by nesting (Card with Avatar slot containing Avatar instance) rather than configuring 25 toggles. Decision rule: ~10 properties is workable; 15-20 is borderline; 25+ is a smell. The composition approach also makes the system more flexible — new card patterns assemble from base pieces rather than waiting for the mega-Card to grow another property. Alternative anti-pattern: hiding properties behind 'mode' variants (Mode=Simple shows 5 props; Mode=Advanced shows 25); this just shifts the complexity rather than reducing it. Real fix is decomposition.

questions.explanation: Split into focused components. 25 props means too much in one component. Composition > configuration overload.
